item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article comes from here and is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surprisingly, my biggest concern in this article is that "no concussive force was observed" when half a trillion liters of air rushed in to fill the sudden void in the world. And how the Foundation knows for sure it's an Einstein-Rosen Bridge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those Special Containment Procedures are really weak. You've got fantastical Einstein-Rosen generating nukes and they don't require any more special care than disassembling them and holding the parts in storage? Even if the intention is to make them identical to nuclear bombs in-universe apart from their detonation, more could be added here. Maybe something where they still have to disassemble it, but whatever material the warhead is made of requires additional procedures.

item.title: Can't wait!
item.content:encoded: Please add the two additional tests when you can! I'm looking forward to there being a biological test to see if they can get something living to survive the test and perhaps one where they send through a beacon with an atomic clock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like you're behind schedule on those autopsies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is really good, but is underrated and lacks information.

This could easily become a top 150 SCP in term of how well it is known, but it needs more effort.

You want to give a good amount of information on the background, but leave just enough information out of the picture to leave one in mystery and wanting more.

A few examples that might help:
SCP-741
SCP-1178
SCP-1745
SCP-1850

I really don't want this to be deleted, but I'm concerned that it may see that faith if it is not improved upon. Keep the charm however!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmmmm not sure about the idea, and I found the execution to be a bit "lacky". No vote for now, will be back later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm reasonably certain that ceasing electrical activity in the body would lead to immediate brain death

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In general, "near dates" (or locations) are vague or obscured as part of the "The pages you read could be taken from any time or place." Unless there is a specific reason to use THAT EXACT date for the ships to show up next month, I (personally) don't see a purpose in it.

Plus the time between the incident and autopsy if far enough separated that forensic evidence would probably be degraded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why? Some articles are located in 2028-2030.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Autopsies on crew remains are scheduled to be completed on 10/15/2015.

Suggest redacting the year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh… it's got potential, but it's just not there yet. Agree with a lot of the above commentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nineteen aerial bombs… (designated SCP-2220-5 to-24)

That makes twenty of them. Please fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was initially hesitant about changing the carrier (since it somewhat addresses the "many worlds theory"), but since the Saratoga in this timeline is already a scip, I've altered the entry to list the Bon Homme Richard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, when I saw the list of naval vessels, I was instantly reminded of 1264.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm neutral on this.

Also, I'm going to note that that the USS Saratoga is already a skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From an execution standpoint, the way the article is written leaves it unclear if it's written from the viewpoint of a universe where Operation Downfall did occur, or if the ships are from an actual alternate reality relative to the document. I think I know what you're intending, but it's not clear immediately to me from the writing.

Conceptually, I do believe that anomalous weaponry in global conflicts is a viable starting point, but this feels too thin for the grand scale you've chosen to employ as a background for your story. Why are wormholes an alternative to nukes? What implications are there from this development? If we're just sending chunks of Japan to alternate realities willy-nilly, surely there's some serious consequences that could result from that? I dunno.

This is light to the point that the article doesn't even really pose the truly interesting questions that arise from your base concept, let alone explore anything that could give the story some weight here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The procedures are better, but they need to describe how the objects need to be contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for the bad taste. I had excised some of the op orders fro the original draft that mentioned that all of the carriers assigned to the Olympic landings had these weapons issued to them, since the casualty estimates were so high on both sides. I may need to re-insert to clarify. Thanks for the note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alternate reality. The idea here is that in their reality, weapons of mass destruction involve creating wormholes rather the nuclear fission. The ships were involved in the invasion of the Japanese home islands-something that was planned but did not take place in our timeline-and an accidental discharge during a repair job on a defective detonator sent the ships caught within the wormhole radius into our reality. Foundation finds the ships, discovers the remaining bombs and locks them away. So far I'm getting that the premise is intriguing but there some kinks in the containment procedures. I do thank all for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USS_Saratoga_(CV-3)#Postwar_years

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USS_Stack_(DD-406)

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USS_Wilson_(DD-408)

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USS_Atascosa_(AO-66)

I looked these naval vessels you mentioned. Just curious are these ships from a different alternate reality different from ours or are these ships from our reality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Inventory manifests indicate that SCP-2220-1 sailed from Pearl Harbor Naval Base on 10/25/1945 with twenty-five devices.

….

This is a little hard to swallow. An aircraft carrier with 25 highly unstable and experimental WMDs casually strolls into the middle of a US naval base and also lists these plausibly classified devices on a manifest? This kills my suspension of disbelief, as it's not very likely this is how a device of this nature would be handled in reality. It makes it feel like you wanted to give this a bit of extra flavor, but it's hitting all the wrong taste buds.

That said, treading into actual history with SCPs is a tricky game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Entire containment section has NOTHING to do with actually containing it.

Note: it's not the concept I have a problem with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2220-5 through -24 have been stored at Secure Armaments Wing, Site-76 with detonator assemblies removed pending further testing.

That is the only thing in your containment procedures that actually do something with how it is currently contained. It is also the last sentence in your procedures. Everything else in the procedures is backstory.

For the containment procedures, we must know ONLY what is needed for keeping the damn thing contained. Everything else is filler, and if there's an emergency, people are gonna die while someone reads through the filler.

Also…

Solutions to Construction Problems

While I'm a fan of "Foundation Fronts with initials of SCP," that's a bit too hammy and forced for my tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…was found to be an abrupt cessation of [DATA REDACTED]

And thar she downvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Curiosity = Peaked.

Think this deserves a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I excised a good amount during the draft phase based on some critiques but I'm thinking some of it should go back in…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, the redacted section basically states that there was a cessation of all electrical activity in the body (due to the mode of transportation) and this caused everyone's heart to stop. Thanks for the feedback, it helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely good, and you managed to catch my interest. I'm going neutral for now though, if only because it just seems like it's missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting, but somehow it doesn't feel quite there yet. No vote so far.

Why did you redact what ceased? I would have assumed it would have been either neurological activity or blood flow, more leaning toward the former as that would indeed cause the heart to stop functioning (along with every other muscle, both voluntary and involuntary). Either way, redacting it doesn't make it any scarier…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely intriguing. Even if it didn't have much of a payoff I'm still giving you a +1.

Edit: Downvoted since then. It's too bare bones at the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the image's description per te.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got it fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because you never uploaded it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure why the pic isn't there but I'm on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image isn't working.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks-working on the photo as we speak. It worked fine in the sandbox. I excised a bit of backstory from it, I may put some back in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You managed to grab my curiosity so I'm compelled to give you a +1.

you really do need to fix that photo though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your image isn't working (yet), and while this had me intrigued, it never went anywhere with it. I fixed your title, by the way.
